video_title: What Does a Super El Niño Mean For the Climate? with Tad Patzek | TGS 225
gist: Earth scientist Tad Patzek argues global warming is now accelerating, driven by a measured decline in Earth's albedo (reflectivity) over the past ~26 years of CERES satellite data, with a likely Super El Niño forming that could spike temperatures past 2C. His preprint projects ~2C of warming by 2050 and >3C by 2100 (≈6C over land), with second-order effects on insurance, infrastructure, water supply, and Global South stability — plus a warning that the shutdown of US (NOAA/NASA) climate data programs is degrading the world's monitoring capability.

insights.claim: Patzek's preprint (in review at Geophysical Research Letters) links an accelerating rate of global warming to a measured ~1% decline in Earth's albedo, from ~0.29 toward ~0.28, observed in 26 years of CERES satellite data — a feedback that, if real, bends the warming curve upward rather than toward a benign plateau.
insights.speaker: Tad Patzek
insights.so_what: A structurally faster warming path raises the tail risk on every climate-exposed asset (coastal real estate, crop yields, P&C insurance) faster than consensus IPCC-style linear models price in; watch albedo/CERES research as a leading indicator.
insights.quote: the Earth has entered a period of an accelerated decline of albedo

insights.claim: Patzek says a simple model using only CO2 forcing (logarithmic), aerosols/land-use/glacier melt (about -1 W/m2), clouds, and the observed albedo change reproduces 140 years of observed planetary temperature 'extremely well,' implying the warming signal is robust and not model-dependent.
insights.speaker: Tad Patzek
insights.so_what: A parsimonious model matching the record undercuts 'the science is uncertain' narratives used to delay climate-adaptation capex and policy — relevant to regulatory and transition-risk timelines.
insights.quote: you actually approximate the real observed temperature of the planet extremely well

insights.claim: Patzek argues warming is approximately linear with cumulative CO2 emissions since ~1850 (not with annual rate), so cutting emissions in one year barely dents the accumulated total unless the annual rate itself is reduced every year — the metric investors and policymakers should track is cumulative, not annual.
insights.speaker: Tad Patzek
insights.so_what: Implies single-year emissions dips (e.g. recessions, COVID) are nearly irrelevant to the warming trajectory; only sustained year-over-year rate cuts matter, reframing carbon-budget and net-zero math.
insights.quote: The temperature of the planet reacts to the cumulative emissions. In fact, the rate almost doesn't matter

themes.theme: Super El Niño and Temperature Spikes

insights.claim: A Super El Niño is probably forming in the Pacific in the coming ~6 months (summer 2026 into fall/winter) and could be one of the strongest in ~140 years, spiking global temperatures potentially to 2C or more above pre-industrial on top of the elevated ~1.5C baseline.
insights.speaker: Tad Patzek
insights.so_what: Near-term El Niño spikes drive powerful cyclones plus drought in Australia/Indonesia/Asia and flooding elsewhere — actionable for agricultural commodity, energy-demand (cooling), and catastrophe-reinsurance positioning within a 6-18 month window.
insights.quote: all indications are that we might... it may be two degrees, it may be more than two degrees

insights.claim: Each El Niño is a temperature excursion above a steadily rising background ocean temperature, so the same oscillations now produce ever more extreme heat domes, droughts, and extreme rainfall even though the system is still mean-reverting to a La Niña/normal state.
insights.speaker: Tad Patzek
insights.so_what: A rising baseline means the tail of weather extremes fattens each cycle — relevant to actuarial repricing of weather risk and to the increasing standard deviation (not just mean) of rainfall and temperature.
insights.quote: these oscillations occur at an ever higher level of global temperature, they will bring ever more extreme events

insights.claim: Patzek warns that present wet conditions in Texas are El Niño-driven moisture from the Pacific amplified by the subtropical jet stream, and that parts of Texas will trend toward hot desert as conditions flip dry and hot.
insights.speaker: Tad Patzek
insights.so_what: Counterintuitive: current wetness is not a reprieve but part of the cycle; signals long-run water-stress and agricultural/real-estate risk for the US South-Central region.
insights.quote: right now be happy. It's wet. It's gonna get dry and hot soon enough

insights.claim: Patzek's central estimate is roughly +2C global average warming by 2050 and a little over +3C by 2100, which translates to about +6C (≈11F) over land — a magnitude he says would render the planet unrecognizable.
insights.speaker: Tad Patzek
insights.so_what: A land-warming figure roughly double the headline global average is far more economically destructive than the commonly cited 2C; investors underwriting multi-decade infrastructure, agriculture, and insurance should stress-test against land-specific numbers.
insights.quote: that range is centered at plus two degrees centigrade of heating in 2050, and a little bit more than three degrees in 2100

insights.claim: The oceans have absorbed ~90% of the excess heat and a third-plus of carbon emissions, and ocean heat absorption accelerated sharply after 2014; this dampens surface warming now but locks in committed warming for decades to centuries even if CO2 injection stopped today.
insights.speaker: Tad Patzek
insights.so_what: Committed warming means adaptation, not just mitigation, is the unavoidable long-duration investment theme; the post-2014 acceleration is a flag that the dampening buffer may be shifting.
insights.quote: even if we stop CO2 injection today, the oce- the ocean still will be warmer

insights.claim: Atmospheric CO2 has risen from ~280 ppm pre-industrial to ~435 ppm today, roughly 46% of emitted CO2 stays in the atmosphere on average, and residence time is ~17-30 years and longer, with concentrations taking centuries to fall back toward 280 ppm if emissions ceased.
insights.speaker: Tad Patzek
insights.so_what: The multi-century persistence of CO2 means transition-risk and adaptation cash flows have a structurally long tail; quantifies why the problem cannot be reversed 'by a stroke of a pen.'
insights.quote: 46% of w- of what we inject stays in the atmosphere on average

insights.claim: Patzek frames climate impact as 'fracturing' rather than 'bending': drought-then-deluge cycles shrink and swell clay soils, breaking infrastructure — roads, bridges, buildings, dams — with dams already failing in many places, implying large mandatory repair/replacement costs globally.
insights.speaker: Tad Patzek
insights.so_what: Direct read-through to infrastructure capex, municipal-bond credit risk, and engineering/construction demand as climate-driven soil cycling degrades built assets across Europe, the Americas, and Asia.
insights.quote: What does it do to your infrastructure... Well, it breaks them. In fact, it fractures them everywhere

insights.claim: Himalayan glaciers, which supply water to about two billion people, may be mostly melted within 30-70 years, producing catastrophic spring meltwater flooding (Pakistan, India, Kashmir, Tibet) followed by deepening summer droughts and eventual loss of drinking-water supply.
insights.speaker: Tad Patzek
insights.so_what: A two-billion-person water-supply cliff is a first-order driver of agricultural disruption, migration, and geopolitical instability across South and East Asia over a multi-decade horizon.
insights.quote: those glaciers supply water to two billion people. Once that water supply disappears, those people have no drinking water

insights.claim: Sea level has already risen ~30 cm since ~1905, and accelerating land-ice melt on Greenland (faster) and Antarctica could each add multiple meters; Patzek cites Jakarta being progressively flooded and slated for relocation to another city.
insights.speaker: Tad Patzek
insights.so_what: Coastal real estate, ports, and sovereign relocation projects (e.g. Indonesia's capital move) become multi-decade dislocations; Greenland's faster melt makes it the nearer-term sea-level driver to monitor.

insights.claim: Permafrost melt in Alaska and Siberia is releasing methane (visible methane craters across Siberia, slumping coasts), a feedback measured in decades to millennia that is not accounted for in baseline projections and that Patzek expects to bend the warming curve up rather than down.
insights.speaker: Tad Patzek
insights.so_what: An under-modeled positive feedback that, if material, invalidates optimistic 'feedbacks bend it down' scenarios and raises the probability of faster-than-budgeted warming.
insights.quote: the feedbacks I suspect are occurring will actually bend it up, which makes our reaction to climate change ever so more urgent

insights.claim: In 2022, officially 62,000 people died of heat waves in Europe in a single year, mostly elderly and vulnerable; comparable statistics do not exist for the US, so heat-exhaustion deaths in Texas, Florida, and the Deep South are unmeasured.
insights.speaker: Tad Patzek
insights.so_what: Quantifies the human/actuarial cost of heat extremes and flags a US data gap; rising heat mortality feeds healthcare, labor-productivity, and life/health-insurance loss assumptions.
insights.quote: in twenty twenty-two, officially 62,000 people died in Europe of heat waves in one year

insights.claim: Patzek documents that in March 2022 East Antarctica hit 40C (70F) above average via a massive Rossby-wave event that collapsed a glacier near Dome C, demonstrating the atmosphere can transport extreme heat from equator to pole — and such events will recur more often.
insights.speaker: Tad Patzek
insights.so_what: Proves the physical mechanism for sudden, extreme regional heat anomalies, raising the probability of low-frequency/high-impact events that catastrophe models may under-weight.
insights.quote: the temperature in Eastern Antarctica became 40 degrees centigrade, 70 degrees Fahrenheit warmer than average

insights.claim: Patzek expects the Indian subcontinent, western/most of China, Pakistan, and Bangladesh to heat far faster than other regions due to El Niño-altered monsoon patterns and Rossby-wave heat transport, raising the odds of a mass-casualty heat-wave die-off in the next 30-50 years.
insights.speaker: Tad Patzek
insights.so_what: Concentrated heat risk in the most populous, lower-income regions points to acute migration, food-security, and political-stability shocks in Asia within an investment-relevant horizon.
insights.quote: the subcontinent of India will be hitting far faster than other areas of the planet

insights.claim: Patzek calls the shutdown of US satellite and measurement programs and the curtailing of public data access at NOAA, NASA, and USDA possibly the largest tragedy in climate understanding, with agencies taking websites down and making data hard to obtain.
insights.speaker: Tad Patzek
insights.so_what: Degraded official US climate data raises uncertainty premiums for any data-dependent risk model (insurance, agriculture, energy) and shifts the informational edge toward those who track alternative sources.
insights.quote: the possibly largest tragedy in our understanding of climate, which is the shutting down of American satellite programs

insights.claim: Patzek says the EU's Copernicus program, plus Japan and increasingly China, are launching more (smaller) satellites to replace aging unreplaced US assets like Aqua and Terra, so the US is being bypassed as the leader in earth-system data; Russian data remains largely inaccessible.
insights.speaker: Tad Patzek
insights.so_what: Leadership in earth-observation data is migrating to the EU and Asia; relevant for the space/earth-observation sector and for which jurisdictions' datasets become the global standard.
insights.quote: the United States will simply be bypassed. Is actually being bypassed

insights.claim: Patzek argues the durable levers on a climate-skeptical, wealthy demographic are greed and fear — concretely framed as home insurability in Florida/California/East Coast, hurricane and flood survival, aquifer salt contamination, grid failure in wet-bulb heat, and access to fuel/groceries.
insights.speaker: Tad Patzek
insights.so_what: Maps the channels through which climate becomes financially salient to households — most concretely property insurance availability/pricing, which is a near-term market signal of climate risk.
insights.quote: you have to appeal to their... to their greed and to their fear

insights.claim: Patzek relays a prominent retired Chevron executive (now a USC professor) who acknowledges climate change but refuses to cut personal consumption, saying 'become rich, because then you can weather any disruption' — which Patzek calls a microcosm of the global elite stance.
insights.speaker: Tad Patzek
insights.so_what: Captures the prevailing elite adaptation strategy — accumulate wealth to buy resilience rather than mitigate — implying continued demand for premium adaptation goods and persistent emissions inertia.
insights.quote: Become rich, because then you can weather any disruption

insights.claim: Patzek's stated near-term research priorities are ocean heat-absorption rate and its effect on currents/heat transport, the north-vs-south direction of albedo change, Antarctic and Greenland ice-melt speed, and Himalayan glacier melt rates.
insights.speaker: Tad Patzek
insights.so_what: A checklist of the variables a climate-watching investor should monitor as the highest-signal leading indicators of how fast physical risk materializes.
